3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Cheng, C. M., Shim, J., Kiss, O., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., He, J., & Baker, F. C. (2024). Bedtime screen use behaviors and sleep outcomes in early adolescents: A prospective cohort study. Journal of Adolescent Health, 75, 650-655.

3C_Exposure.exposure_measure: Screen Usage Around Bedtime questionnaire (9-item) plus additional sleep-related screen use items; total recreational screen time from Youth Screen Time Survey
3C_Exposure.how_operationalized: Bedtime screen activities rated on 5-point Likert scale (1=never to 5=every night) in past week at Year 2; phone behavior at bedtime (turn off, silent/vibrate, leave ringer on, outside room); TV/electronic device in bedroom (yes/no); total recreational screen time in hours/day (weighted weekday/weekend average)

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Sleep outcomes (sleep disturbance, disorders of initiating/maintaining sleep, weekly sleep duration)
3D_Outcome.measure_used: Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children (caregiver-reported, 26-item, binary cutoff of 39); disorders of initiating and maintaining sleep subscale; Munich Chronotype Questionnaire (self-reported sleep duration, weighted weekday/weekend average)
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: 25.5% had total sleep disturbance at Year 3

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type: Logistic regression (sleep disturbance) and multiple linear regression (sleep duration, disorders subscale)
3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Logistic regression for binary sleep disturbance outcome; ordinal logistic regression for disorders of initiating/maintaining sleep subscale; multiple linear regression for weekly sleep duration. All adjusted for confounders. Propensity weights applied from American Community Survey.
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: Age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, study site, adverse childhood experiences, melatonin use, depression symptoms (CBCL t-score), sleep variable at Year 2
3E_Design_Analysis.SES_in_model: yes - household income and parent education included as covariates
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Logistic regression models were used to estimate the associations between screen usage around bedtime and overall screen use at Year 2 and binarized total sleep disturbance at Year 3, adjusting for confounders and total sleep disturbance at Year 2.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: TV/internet device in bedroom -> weekly sleep duration (hours)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Having a TV or electronic device in the bedroom was prospectively associated with shorter weekly sleep duration (B = -0.21; 95% confidence interval [CI] -0.28, -0.13).

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Leave ringer on (vs turn phone off) -> sleep disturbance (binary)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Leaving the ringer on, compared to turning the phone off, was prospectively associated with sleep disturbance (binary) (odds ratio [OR] = 1.25; 95% CI 1.00, 1.56)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Leave ringer on (vs turn phone off) -> weekly sleep duration
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: and shorter weekly sleep duration (B = -0.27; 95% CI -0.40, -0.13)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Ringer on silent/vibrate (vs turn phone off) -> weekly sleep duration
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: putting the ringer on silent or vibrate, compared to turning the phone off, was prospectively associated with shorter weekly sleep duration (B = -0.15; 95% CI -0.26, -0.05).

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Play video games at bedtime -> sleep disturbance
Play music at bedtime -> sleep disturbance
Talk on phone/text at bedtime -> sleep disturbance
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Talk on the phone or text OR 1.13 (1.05, 1.23), p = .002

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Spend time on social media at bedtime -> sleep disturbance
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Spend time online on social media (e.g., Facebook) OR 1.10 (1.01, 1.20), p = .025

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Spend time on social media at bedtime -> weekly sleep duration
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Spend time online on social media... weekly sleep duration B = -0.21 (-0.26, -0.16), p < .001

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Play video games at bedtime -> weekly sleep duration
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Play video games... weekly sleep duration B = -0.14 (-0.19, -0.08), p < .001

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Talk on phone/text at bedtime -> weekly sleep duration
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Talk on the phone or text... weekly sleep duration B = -0.17 (-0.22, -0.12), p < .001

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Total recreational screen time -> sleep disturbance
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Total Recreational Screen Time OR 1.02 (1.01, 1.04), p = .002

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Total recreational screen time -> weekly sleep duration
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Total Recreational Screen Time... weekly sleep duration B = -0.03 (-0.04, -0.02), p < .001
3F_Effect_Sizes.below_threshold: N/A - unstandardized B but very small magnitude

3G_Interpretation.verbatim_recommendation: Clinicians can consider inquiring about bedtime screen use, especially in early adolescence, and provide education to parents and adolescents on bedtime screen use. Parents can consider implementing a Family Media Use Plan, which could include limiting screen use before bedtime.

3H_Rhetorical.pattern_detail: The paper leads with statistical significance throughout. ORs for individual bedtime activities predicting sleep disturbance are very close to 1.00 (1.10-1.13 per unit on Likert scale) and the total screen time OR is 1.02, yet these are presented as meaningful prospective associations warranting clinical recommendations about limiting screen use. The paper does not contextualize the very small magnitude of these effects.

3I_Flags.ambiguity_notes: Effect sizes for the bedtime-specific activities are per-unit increases on a 5-point Likert scale, so ORs of 1.10 represent small incremental effects. The total recreational screen time OR of 1.02 per hour/day is particularly tiny. Unstandardized B coefficients for sleep duration are in hours, so -0.03 hours per hour of screen time is approximately 1.8 minutes less sleep. The paper notes effects are 'weaker in magnitude' for total screen time vs bedtime-specific screen use but does not explicitly label any effects as small.
